draft.introduction: Calcidius (4th century CE) produced the only Latin translation of Plato’s Timaeus available in the medieval West, along with an extensive commentary. His work transmitted Platonic cosmology to the Latin world and shaped how medieval thinkers understood creation, matter, and the world-soul.

translation.text: Matter is not evil in itself, as the Gnostics suppose, but is the receptacle and nurse of becoming, as Plato teaches. It receives all forms without possessing any character of its own. The Demiurge acts upon matter not by force but by persuasion, bringing order to what was disordered. Silva, which the Greeks call hyle, is neither good nor evil but the condition of possibility for the created world.
translation.original_text: Silva igitur, quam Graeci hylen vocant, neque bona neque mala est, sed possibilitatis condicio.

translator_notes.note: Calcidius’s use of silva (forest, wood) to translate Plato’s hyle (matter) gave the Latin West its standard term for primordial matter. His defense of matter’s neutrality against Gnostic dualism was crucial for the development of Christian cosmology.
